title: Who decides when AI is too dangerous?
slides: Who decides when AI becomes too risky?
Not governments—tech companies set the limits.
Some firms even skip safety checks before launch.
You lose trust in every update they push.
Demand external audits for all major AI releases.
thread_text:
1. Who sets the risk limit?

Spotify fired its entire AI safety team six months before shipping a new feature that generated fake listener comments.

2. No external tests required

Google's Gemini launched without a third-party security review. The company ran its own tests instead.

3. Trust vanishes instantly

When users discovered a model generating false legal citations, they started doubting every official update.

4. The real cost is invisible

Your personal data gets ingested into models without your knowledge. There is no opt-out button.

5. Demand a public audit now

Ask your member of parliament to mandate independent safety audits for all commercial AI systems by the end of this year.
video_script: Tech companies decide when AI is too risky.
Some skip safety checks before they launch.
Every broken update erodes your trust.
Demand independent oversight for every new model.

thread:

script: Tech companies decide when AI is too risky.
Some skip safety checks before they launch.
Every broken update erodes your trust.
Demand independent oversight for every new model.

instagram.caption:
Spotify signed off on a feature that generated fake listener comments 6 months after firing its entire AI safety team. 😶

Google’s Gemini launched without a single external security test—just an internal sign-off. No outside auditor ever looked at the code. No one had to.

If you publish or create with AI tools, you are trusting the same companies that skip these checks with your content—and your audience’s trust.

Who is holding the company that powers your workflow accountable for the next update?
